interactions.summary: Jiang continues his biography: Yale felt like an achievement factory rather than a home for intellectual curiosity, so he looked for Chinese roots, returned to China in 1999, tried education reform, then moved into geopolitical prediction as an Asimov/Foundation-inspired experiment in historical pattern recognition.

Jay asks Jiang to clarify that he is not technically a professor, and Jiang explains the moniker: Yale B.A. only, high school Great Books teacher, classroom uploads, and viewers adopting the title as an online sign of respect.
Jay sets aside credentialism and asks how Jiang assesses the accuracy of his political-prophet or Nostradamus-style reputation.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 is Jiang; SPEAKER_01 is Jay. seg-0024 begins Jay's next substantive question and continues into packet 4.

claims.claim: Jiang says Yale disappointed him because it was oriented toward success, law school, Wall Street, and money rather than open intellectual curiosity.
claims.temporal_scope: Biographical interpretation of Yale years, stated 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang says he returned to China in 1999, has been there for nearly thirty years, and originally wanted to promote education reform through reading and critical thinking.
claims.temporal_scope: Biographical chronology through 1999 and after, stated 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang says his geopolitical videos began a couple of years before the interview as a playful test of whether historical patterns could be deciphered and used to predict events such as Iran, American civil war, and Trump's 2024 victory.
claims.temporal_scope: Account of prediction project beginnings around 2024, given 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang's 'Professor' title is an internet moniker rather than an institutional credential; he identifies his formal schooling as a Yale College literature B.A. and his work as high school Great Books teaching.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang links predictive history to Asimov's Foundation: a childhood attraction to psychohistory becomes a public experiment in whether history has decipherable patterns.
signature_moments.source_phrase: are there certain patterns to history
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It locates Jiang's prediction method in a literary imagination before it becomes political analysis.

signature_moments.moment: After the painful biography, Jiang punctures the rise with deadpan irony: now he is world famous and everyone hates him.
signature_moments.source_phrase: now I'm world famous. And everyone hates me
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The line condenses internet fame as another form of bullying rather than uncomplicated success.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's practice of asking whether historical patterns can be deciphered, inspired by Isaac Asimov's Foundation series.
An internet moniker adopted after viewers used it for Jiang's high school Great Books lecture uploads, not an academic title.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates his return to China to 1999 and his pivot into geopolitical prediction videos to roughly two years before the 2026-04-01 interview.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang's quoted viewer rationale for the Professor title is anecdotal self-report, not independent audience research.
